displayTitle: The Sensor That Reads the Field Itself
kicker: ET LEVEL I // HALL EFFECT SENSORS

beats.narration: Every coil senses a field by induction, so it reads nothing unless the flux is changing. The Hall element breaks that rule.

payload.title: TWO WAYS TO LISTEN
payload.sub: A coil hears the field change. A Hall element hears the field itself.

beats.narration: Drive a steady current through a thin semiconductor chip in a perpendicular field. The Lorentz force shoves carriers sideways until a transverse Hall voltage appears.
That voltage tracks flux density at any frequency, so a Hall element reads a static DC field with polarity, telling north from south.

payload.heading: What a Hall element gives you

beats.narration: A coil's output falls with frequency and dies at DC, so the two trade places at a crossover. Below it, Hall wins.

payload.heading: Where the Hall element earns its place

chips.s: Coil output collapses; Hall stays flat
Reading is independent of scan speed

beats.narration: One line carries the lesson. A coil measures the rate of change of flux; a Hall element measures the flux itself.

payload.sub: It still replaces the detector, not the excitation coil.
